title: Mariscus asper
intro: Mariscus asper, also known as the rough-leaved marsh cattail or rough cattail, is a perennial aquatic plant that belongs to the sedge family (Cyperaceae). The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, making it useful in treating various health conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Mariscus asper include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its pharmacological effects. The plant is commonly used in traditional medicine and is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is also used in the production of herbal teas and ointments.
taxonomy: Mariscus asper is classified as a species of fern in the family Thelypteridaceae, with the genus Mariscus being part of the larger family of ferns. It belongs to the kingdom Plantae, the division Pteridophyta, and the class Polypodiopsida. The species Mariscus asper is native to tropical regions, specifically in Africa. Mariscus asper is also known as the Rough-leaved Button Fern, which gives insight into its physical characteristics. The classification of Mariscus asper provides a clear understanding of its place in the botanical world.
